slides.title: Who Will Control the AI Infrastructure of Higher Education?
slides.subtitle: From useful tools to structural dependence, and how universities can still shape their future
slides.author: Dr Stuart Grey
slides.badge: Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026

notes.transition: Open with the question, not with housekeeping.
points: Frame the keynote as a shift from 'how should we use AI?' to 'who controls the infrastructure we are starting to rely on?'
Say this is a governance talk for a room already thinking about pedagogy, assessment, and practice.
Flag that the talk is neither anti-AI nor anti-industry; it is about stewardship.

notes.transition: Move from the big question to why I am the person making this argument.
points: Keep this very brief: this is not biography for its own sake.
Use it to establish that the perspective comes from both technical AI work and practical HE institutional work.
Set up the next two slides as the two halves of that perspective.

notes.transition: Take the first half of that perspective: technical grounding.
points: Use this to show I have a machine learning / AI research background, not just a policy interest.
The point is that I understand AI systems as systems with architecture, coordination, and failure modes.
Do not dwell on the thesis details; translate it into credibility for seeing the stack.

slides.title: Free Text Analysis with Student Voice AI

notes.transition: Then pivot from technical grounding to applied institutional grounding.
points: Explain that most of my day-to-day work is free-text analysis with UK education institutions through Student Voice AI.
This means I see what AI and data practices look like inside real institutional workflows, not only in theory.
Say explicitly that the talk comes from looking at both the technology and the institutional consequences.

notes.transition: Now give the room the first of three lenses for reading the rest of the talk.
points: Transparency asks: what can institutions actually see, inspect, explain, and contest?
Use it to frame provenance, auditability, model dependencies, data use, and institutional legibility.
Deliver it as a clean beat, not a full explanation yet.

notes.transition: Add the second lens by shifting from visibility to shape.
points: Distortion asks what gets bent when systems reward what is easy to capture, measure, or automate.
This will later connect to assessment design, pedagogy, workflow simplification, and institutional incentives.
Keep the rhythm deliberate so the triad lands.

notes.transition: Complete the triad with the political-economy lens.
points: Extraction asks who captures value when educational activity becomes data, workflow, or dependency.
Later this will connect to data use, vendor leverage, rent streams, and platform power.
Make clear that these are not three separate topics; they are three ways of seeing the same infrastructure.

slides.text: The AI argument in higher education is no longer mainly about whether the tools are impressive. It is about who sets the terms of dependence.

notes.transition: Now make the frame explicit and turn the lenses into the keynote thesis.
points: Say that the argument is no longer mainly about whether the tools are impressive.
Say the deeper question is who sets the terms of dependence as AI becomes normal infrastructure.
Use this as the bridge from the opening frame into the body of the talk.

slides.title: From tool to substrate
slides.subtitle: How AI moved from optional assistance to background infrastructure

notes.transition: Having set up the lenses, move into the first descriptive claim.
points: This section explains how AI moved from visible tool to background infrastructure.
Use the subtitle to tell the audience we are now moving from framing to diagnosis.
The job here is to make 'infrastructure' feel concrete.

slides.heading: AI is moving from visible tools to invisible systems
slides.body: The important shift is not simply more campus use. It is that AI is being stitched into teaching, assessment, student support, research workflows and institutional decision-making. When that happens, AI stops looking like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.

notes.transition: Start with the broad institutional picture before going to evidence.
points: AI is no longer just a tool someone opens; it is becoming part of how teaching, assessment, support, research, and governance are carried out.
Stress the shift from optional use to embedded workflow.
Say that once many activities assume the system is there, AI stops behaving like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.
Teaching and assessment: Generative tools now sit inside drafting, feedback, marking support and redesign of assessment.
Student support and admin: Copilots, triage systems and analytics are creeping into advising, helpdesks and routine services.
Research and governance: Search, synthesis, coding, grant development and management reporting increasingly run through AI-mediated layers.

slides.heading: Student adoption is already mainstream
slides.body: The governance problem is immediate because student practice has already normalised faster than institutional structures have adapted.

stats.label: of UK full-time undergraduates use AI in at least one way
use generative AI to help with assessed work
say assessment has changed significantly because of AI

notes.transition: Back the previous claim with numbers so the room feels the urgency.
points: Use the HEPI figures to show this is already mainstream student practice.
Link the assessment figure directly to the conference context: the room already knows assessment is changing.
The point is not surprise at student use; the point is that institutional governance can no longer lag behind it.

slides.heading: Governance is lagging behind everyday use
slides.body: Universities now face a familiar problem: mass uptake at the edge, patchy coordination at the centre, and inconsistent messaging in between. That is a recipe for drift.

notes.transition: Move from mainstream use to institutional response.
points: Mass adoption at the edge with patchy coordination at the centre is the governance problem.
Emphasise fragmentation: policy, capability, and messaging are uneven.
If useful, say that this is exactly how institutions drift into dependence without ever formally deciding to.
Fragmented policy: Many institutions are still experimenting with guidance rather than operating settled governance.
Uneven capability: Staff literacy, procurement confidence and evaluation capacity vary widely across campuses.
Ambiguous signals: Students are told AI matters, but often receive little clear support about how, when or why to use it.

slides.heading: Most infrastructure is invisible until it breaks
slides.body: The easiest way to see infrastructure is through failure. Outages, upstream model changes, or jurisdictional pressure make clear just how much teaching and institutional continuity already depend on remote systems.

notes.transition: Now make infrastructure tangible by talking about failure.
points: Most infrastructure feels abstract until an outage, upstream model change, or legal/jurisdictional issue exposes it.
Use failure as the simplest way to show how much continuity already depends on remote systems.
This is where the word 'infrastructure' should start to feel real to the audience.
Service continuity: When cloud-linked platforms fail, content, assignments, grades and communication fail with them.
Upstream dependency: A change made by a provider can alter outputs, reliability or availability without the university changing anything locally.
Sovereignty and resilience: Once core services sit elsewhere, governance is also about jurisdiction, coercion and the ability to keep operating under stress.

slides.text: Once AI becomes part of the substrate, the question shifts from capability to control.

notes.transition: Compress the section into a single line and let it land.
points: The takeaway is a conceptual shift: from asking what AI can do to asking who controls the conditions under which it operates.
Pause slightly here; this is one of the key hinge lines of the talk.
Use it to introduce the first practical implication: governance has to become a system, not just a sentiment.

slides.heading: Universities need a viable system for AI governance, not just a policy
slides.body: Many institutions already have local experimentation. What they often lack is coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system.

notes.transition: Before leaving the opening diagnosis, turn it into the first institutional implication.
points: If governance is lagging behind use, the answer cannot be a thin guidance document.
The institution needs coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system.
Use this as the first practical move so the talk does not leave action until the end.

slides.title: The build-out
slides.subtitle: Why the boom matters even if the hype does not last

notes.transition: Having sketched the first implication, zoom back out to the upstream build-out behind it.
points: This section moves from campus use to the political economy of the wider stack.
Signal that the next slides are not dismissing AI as hype; they are asking what durable capacity the boom is creating, and for whom.
This is the bridge from institutional dependence to the infrastructure being built upstream.

slides.heading: Speculative capital is building real capacity
slides.body: The current AI boom is not just hype. It is also an enormous build-out of compute, models, software tooling and enterprise integration. Even if many bets fail, the capacity being built will not simply disappear.

notes.transition: Open the section by separating hype from infrastructure.
points: The key line is that speculative capital can still build real capacity.
Point to compute, models, tooling, and integrations as durable inheritances of the current boom.
Say that the question is not whether infrastructure is being built; it is who will control what is left behind.
Compute and cloud: Data centres, GPU fleets and inference infrastructure are expanding at extraordinary speed.
Tooling and standards: Evaluation suites, orchestration frameworks, safety tooling and deployment patterns are being hardened quickly.
Organisational learning: The frenzy is forcing universities and vendors alike to learn fast, even through messy pilots.

slides.heading: The upstream build-out is vast — and highly concentrated
slides.body: The boom is leaving behind real infrastructure, but that infrastructure is being financed and controlled by a remarkably small set of actors.

stats.label: top three cloud providers' share of the public-cloud market in 2025
Alphabet's signalled 2026 capital expenditure, largely for technical infrastructure
strategic AI partnerships and GPU commitments shaping the frontier stack

notes.transition: Now show the scale and concentration numerically.
points: Use these figures to make the upstream stack feel vast and unevenly owned.
The point is not memorising exact numbers; it is seeing how far beyond single-university scale this market operates.
This helps explain why universities are mostly downstream actors unless they collaborate strategically.

slides.heading: The build-out can leave universities with real inheritances
slides.body: If institutions are strategic, the boom years can amount to a temporary subsidy from speculative capital into durable capability that HE later uses more cheaply and more wisely.

notes.transition: Keep the section from sounding fatalistic.
points: Say that boom periods can leave useful inheritances if universities are deliberate.
Translate that inheritance into practical terms: cheaper access later, better institutional tooling, and more credible alternatives.
This is where you show you are not anti-development; you are anti-passivity.
Cheaper access later: Competition, commoditisation and maturing open models can drive down cost and increase choice.
Better institutional tooling: Standards for VLE integration, evaluation, monitoring and retrieval are improving on someone else's budget.
More credible alternatives: Mature open-weight models and sector hosting become more plausible once the ecosystem has been pushed forward.

slides.heading: The boom is also accelerating learning inside universities
slides.body: The real gain is not just hardware. It is the organisational knowledge institutions can build while the ecosystem is moving quickly.

notes.transition: Shift from external capital to internal learning.
points: The boom is not only changing the market; it is forcing institutions to learn fast.
Point to policy, pedagogy, and organisational capability as things universities can build during this period.
The implication is that institutions can waste the moment or use it to become better stewards.
Policy learning: Assessment guidance, data rules, DPIAs and usage norms are being stress-tested in real time.
Pedagogic learning: Universities are discovering which AI uses support learning and which merely simulate support.
Capability learning: Staff are developing literacy in prompting, workflow design, evaluation and critical comparison across tools.

slides.heading: But infrastructure built in a frenzy can still produce dependency
slides.body: Build-outs do not guarantee healthy outcomes. The same boom that creates useful capacity can also create habits of lock-in, misallocated effort and narrow models of progress.

notes.transition: Then add the caution.
points: A boom can create dependency as easily as it creates capability.
Emphasise generosity, convenience, and rapid integration as the conditions under which lock-in often begins.
This is the place to say that infrastructure built in a frenzy often embeds someone else's priorities.
Lock-in during generosity: Deep discounts and rapid integrations make dependence feel painless while switching costs are low and still rising.
Misplaced priorities: Leaders can chase visible partnerships and shiny features while neglecting pedagogy, people and core digital resilience.
Capture of norms: The first systems to scale often end up defining what counts as modern, efficient or inevitable.

slides.text: The boom can be a subsidy for universities, if universities do not confuse access with control.

notes.transition: Land the contrast in one phrase.
points: The distinction is access versus control.
Say that temporary access to powerful systems is not the same as governing the terms on which they become normal.
Use this line to pivot into the next section: if not universities, who is setting the defaults?

notes.transition: Now move from capacity to power.
points: This section asks who actually sets the defaults embedded in the stack.
Use the subtitle to signal that the issue is not only lock-in but agenda-setting and mission drift.
We are moving from 'how much is being built?' to 'whose assumptions shape what gets built?'

slides.heading: A small number of firms increasingly control the stack
slides.body: AI is not arriving in higher education as a neutral marketplace of interchangeable tools. It is arriving through a concentrated stack of models, cloud, productivity suites, learning platforms and distribution channels.

notes.transition: Start with the stack itself.
points: Explain that universities are not buying from a flat market of interchangeable tools.
Show the layered dependence: model providers, cloud providers, and interface/platform providers.
The audience needs to see that concentration exists across multiple layers at once.
Model layer: A handful of providers dominate frontier model access and shape the pace of release.
Infrastructure layer: Cloud concentration gives the same firms leverage over cost, continuity and technical standards.
Interface layer: Existing campus software ecosystems become the channel through which AI is normalised.

slides.heading: Technology does not choose its own social purpose
slides.body: New systems do not automatically produce broad progress. The gains depend on who gets to decide what the tools are for, which kinds of work they replace, and who captures the surplus they create.

notes.transition: Then make the normative move.
points: Technology does not arrive with its social purpose already settled.
Stress that who decides the purpose of a system matters as much as what the system can do.
This is a good place to quietly introduce the idea that architecture and incentives shape educational outcomes.
Direction matters: AI can be used to remove drudgery and expand human capability, or to centralise control and reduce labour.
Bargaining power matters: Without strong institutions, efficiency narratives can define the agenda before educators and students get a say.
Narratives matter: The story that 'AI is inevitable' can make resistance to bad design sound backward rather than responsible.

slides.heading: In universities, the first question is whose goals are being encoded
slides.body: Most institutional AI projects appear to be about productivity, innovation or student experience. But beneath those labels lie different interests that do not always align.

notes.transition: Bring the argument fully into higher education.
points: Ask whose goals are being encoded when institutions talk about productivity, innovation, or student experience.
Contrast management incentives, vendor incentives, and academic/student interests without caricaturing any of them.
The point is that those interests overlap imperfectly, so governance cannot be assumed.
Management incentives: Financial pressure makes cost-saving and scalability tempting default objectives.
Vendor incentives: The platform wants adoption, telemetry, stickiness and cross-product integration.
Academic and student interests: Universities are supposed to privilege learning, judgment, accessibility, care and public value.

slides.heading: The deepest risk is not only lock-in but agenda-setting
slides.body: Once universities depend on upstream systems, external firms gain quiet influence over what counts as good teaching infrastructure, sensible research workflows and acceptable institutional analytics.

notes.transition: Deepen the risk from lock-in to agenda-setting.
points: Say the deepest problem is not only being unable to leave; it is having external actors quietly shape what counts as sensible teaching and governance.
Use research, pedagogy, and ethical narrowing as examples of how defaults steer institutions.
This is where the talk becomes clearly about power, not just procurement.
Research agenda-setting: Universities increasingly build on proprietary models rather than shaping the architecture, objectives or evaluation criteria.
Pedagogic agenda-setting: Teaching begins to bend toward what the tool can easily generate, score or measure.
Ethical narrowing: Safety and compliance get more attention than labour, justice, pluralism or democratic control.

slides.heading: The frontier is upstream; universities are mostly downstream users
slides.body: The scale of the companies shaping the AI stack helps explain why universities cannot assume their values will be reflected automatically.

stats.label: of notable AI models in 2024 came from industry
notable models in 2024 were produced by US institutions, far ahead of other national systems
Microsoft reported Azure annual revenue above this level in FY25

notes.transition: Use the numbers to show why universities are mostly downstream.
points: Industry dominance in models and hyperscaler scale explain the asymmetry.
This is not a reason to give up; it is a reason to know where leverage has to come from.
Say that universities will rarely win by outspending the frontier, but they can still govern how they connect to it.

slides.heading: Mission drift enters institutions through perfectly reasonable procurement
slides.body: Universities rarely decide to abandon their mission. They drift when sensible short-term deals gradually rewrite the system around external priorities.

notes.transition: Translate the structural argument into ordinary institutional behaviour.
points: Mission drift usually arrives through sensible local decisions, not dramatic declarations.
Bundled convenience, default pathways, and contract terms rewrite the environment gradually.
This slide should make the risk feel familiar rather than ideological.
Bundled convenience: AI arrives inside familiar productivity suites and platforms, making deeper dependency feel incremental.
Default pathways: Institution-wide licences quietly define which tools staff and students see as normal or approved.
Contractual gravity: Once workflows, content and reporting are built into one stack, future decisions become less free than they appear.

slides.text: If universities plug into the stack on vendor terms, the future arrives as infrastructure before it arrives as debate.
slides.highlight: arrives as infrastructure before it arrives as debate

notes.transition: Let the central warning crystallise.
points: This is one of the strongest lines in the deck, so give it air.
The point is that infrastructure settles before institutions have fully debated its consequences.
Use it to introduce a procurement implication: the way universities buy now will shape the future they are still able to contest.

slides.heading: Buy infrastructure like it will one day disappoint you
slides.body: Good procurement assumes that every platform will eventually change price, terms, quality or strategic direction. The question is whether the university remains able to respond.

notes.transition: Having shown how vendor terms shape the future, give the room a procurement rule.
points: Treat procurement as governance rather than as a back-office purchase.
Assume every platform will eventually change price, terms, quality or direction.
The practical test is whether portability, open standards and exit planning are preserved from the start.

notes.transition: After the procurement implication, move back into what dependence actually does inside the university.
points: This section asks what dependence changes in pedagogy, labour, data, and inequality.
Signal that the next slides are about educational consequences, not just market structure.
This is where the three opening lenses start paying off most clearly.

slides.heading: The crucial divide is between augmentation and substitution
slides.body: The most important design decision is not whether AI is present. It is whether AI is used to amplify human expertise or to thin it out.

notes.transition: Open with the central distinction.
points: The most important divide is augmentation versus substitution.
Say that the question is not whether AI is present, but what kind of relationship to human expertise the institution is normalising.
This becomes the test you keep applying to the rest of the examples.
Augmentation: AI can remove admin burden, scaffold formative feedback and support accessibility so staff can focus on judgment and contact.
Substitution: AI can also be used to replace teaching presence, automate first-pass support and standardise assessment in thinner ways.
Institutional test: Every use case should be judged by whether it improves educational experience and professional autonomy, not merely cost.

slides.heading: The wrong uses are easy to scale
slides.body: Universities under pressure are especially vulnerable to AI uses that look efficient because they industrialise surface tasks while hollowing out the human work students actually value.

notes.transition: Now name the tempting failure mode.
points: The worst uses are attractive because they scale quickly under financial and workload pressure.
Examples like industrialised content, automated feedback, and surveillance systems look efficient while thinning educational value.
Say this is where distortion shows up most clearly.
Content at scale: AI-authored slides, AI-voiced lectures and thin content automation can expand provision while shrinking teaching presence.
Automated feedback: Fully automated marking and commentary promise speed but can reduce contestability, nuance and trust.
Surveillance systems: Student-success dashboards and risk scoring can become monitoring infrastructure rather than support infrastructure.

slides.heading: The better uses are harder — but more valuable
slides.body: The most defensible AI applications are often the ones that complement human work rather than attempt to replace it.

notes.transition: Then keep the talk balanced by naming the better uses.
points: The most defensible uses usually complement human work instead of replacing it.
Stress admin relief, accessibility, and formative scaffolding as examples of augmentation.
This helps prevent the argument being heard as blanket resistance.
Admin relief: Triage, drafting, search and routine coordination can free staff time for high-value conversation.
Accessibility and inclusion: Translation, captioning, alternative formats and scaffolded support can widen participation when designed well.
Formative scaffolds: AI can support low-stakes practice and feedback that academics then interpret, deepen and challenge.

slides.heading: Pedagogy starts to bend around what the platform can measure
slides.body: Once AI tools are tightly integrated, course design can start adapting to the system rather than the other way round. That is how commercial logic becomes pedagogic logic.

notes.transition: Bring the distortion lens directly into pedagogy.
points: Platforms nudge teaching toward what they can count, standardise, and render visible.
Say that over time pedagogy can start adapting to platform logic rather than educational purpose.
This is a subtle but central claim: metrics and affordances become curricular pressure.
Standardisation pressure: Assessments and activities may drift toward formats that are easy to auto-score, summarise or monitor.
Surface metrics: Completion, engagement and usage are privileged because they are measurable, even when they are weak proxies for learning.
Partner visibility: Platforms and publishers can become the default route through which content, prompts and resources are surfaced.

slides.heading: Data becomes a new rent stream
slides.body: Universities sit on rich corpora of writing, interaction and workflow data. Once AI layers are integrated, those traces become commercially valuable far beyond their immediate educational use.

notes.transition: Now bring in extraction.
points: Educational data does not only support services; it can become a rent stream.
Explain behavioural exhaust, model improvement, and upstream value capture in plain institutional terms.
The room should hear that data governance is about political economy, not only compliance.
Behavioural exhaust: Prompts, drafts, clicks, revisions and case interactions all become telemetry.
Institutional dependency: The model that best 'knows' how your university writes and works may be one you do not own.
Upstream capture: Value created from public educational activity can be turned into private rent streams.

slides.heading: Capacity gaps are opening inside the sector
slides.body: The danger is not only that everyone gets the same AI. It is that the best human-plus-AI arrangements go to those with the most money, skill and bargaining power.

stats.label: in HEPI 2025, only this share agreed their institution encouraged AI use
reported institutional AI skills training in the same survey
yet by 2026, AI use of some form was already mainstream among full-time undergraduates

notes.transition: Use this slide to show unevenness inside the sector.
points: The risk is not only sameness; it is stratification.
Institutions with more money, skill, and bargaining power will get better human-plus-AI arrangements.
That makes stewardship a sector issue as well as an institutional one.

slides.heading: Research and knowledge production are affected too
slides.body: Once disciplines depend on proprietary models, higher education risks shifting from designing and interrogating tools to adapting around opaque systems made elsewhere.

notes.transition: Widen the argument from teaching to knowledge production.
points: Research workflows are affected when disciplines depend on proprietary systems built elsewhere.
Point to methodological narrowing, reduced pluralism, and legitimacy transfer.
The takeaway is that dependence changes not only delivery but also how knowledge gets made.
Methodological narrowing: Research workflows can default to one model's guardrails, training data and blind spots.
Reduced pluralism: Open, local or public-interest models struggle for attention when corporate stacks are easiest to deploy.
Legitimacy transfer: Universities can become testing grounds and legitimacy providers for tools they did not shape.

slides.text: The issue is not that AI enters teaching and research. The issue is how much educational purpose gets rewritten by commercial defaults.

notes.transition: State the educational core of the argument.
points: The concern is not that AI enters higher education; that is already happening.
The concern is how much educational purpose gets rewritten by commercial defaults.
Use this to introduce the data-governance implication: educational traces cannot just slide upstream by default.

slides.heading: Treat educational data as a governed asset, not free raw material
slides.body: Student work, support data and interaction traces are mission-governed resources. They should not drift into open-ended upstream model improvement by default.

notes.transition: After naming distortion and extraction in teaching and research, make the data implication explicit.
points: Student work, support data and interaction traces are governed assets, not free raw material.
Separate operational delivery, evaluation, and model-improvement uses rather than letting them blur together.
This is where student rights and institutional legitimacy become concrete.

notes.transition: After the data implication, move from effects to process.
points: This section explains how convenience hardens into dependence over time.
Say that platforms rarely begin with a visible squeeze; they usually begin with generosity.
The audience should recognise a lifecycle, not a conspiracy.

slides.heading: The first phase of every platform feels unusually generous
slides.body: Early-stage platform ecosystems often look like public goods. Prices are low, credits are plentiful, integrations are rapid and the user experience is genuinely helpful.

notes.transition: Start the lifecycle with the attractive phase.
points: Early-stage platforms often feel like public goods: low prices, credits, rapid integration, real usefulness.
Do not sneer at this phase; acknowledge that the value is often genuine.
The point is that early generosity is exactly what makes later dependence possible.
Subsidised access: Free tiers, education discounts and pilot credits make experimentation feel costless.
Rapid value: Drafting, summarising, coding support and search assistance can deliver immediate productivity gains.
Soft terms: Data clauses and dependencies feel abstract because the practical benefits are visible now.

slides.heading: Then the real customer starts to shift
slides.body: As the platform matures, optimisation moves away from students and staff as users toward institutions, intermediaries and upstream business strategy.

notes.transition: Then identify the subtle shift.
points: Over time, optimisation moves toward the platform's strategic customer and business model.
Users still experience value, but the system is increasingly shaped around telemetry, procurement scale, and revenue logic.
This is the beginning of distortion at ecosystem level.
Telemetry value: Student and staff behaviour becomes raw material for model improvement, analytics and product design.
Buyer logic: Enterprise customers are offered dashboards, nudges and metrics that serve management priorities first.
Behaviour shaping: The platform starts 'twiddling the knobs' to improve monetisation, retention or partner visibility.

slides.heading: Then the institutional squeeze begins
slides.body: Once the university is deeply integrated, the platform can change the terms of value capture without needing to make the system obviously unusable.

notes.transition: Now bring in the squeeze.
points: Once the institution is integrated, value capture can be rebalanced through pricing, features, and terms.
The platform does not have to become unusable to become more extractive.
Say that this is why governance has to happen before dependence becomes normal.
Price ratchets: Per-seat AI costs and premium tiers rise as migration becomes more disruptive.
Paywalled capability: Features that become pedagogically important move behind higher-priced plans.
Proprietary dependency: AI-authored content, tuned workflows and analytics histories live inside hard-to-export systems.

slides.heading: Exit becomes expensive because the institution has already adapted
slides.body: The deepest switching cost is not technical. It is social and organisational. By the time the squeeze is felt, the university has already reorganised itself around the platform.

notes.transition: Explain why leaving is hard.
points: The deepest switching cost is not technical; it is organisational.
By the time institutions want to exit, workflows, expectations, staff habits, and data structures have already adapted.
That is why exit rights have to be preserved early.
Workflow lock-in: Assessment routines, support scripts, integration logic and approval pathways already assume one stack.
Capability lock-in: Staff training, user habits and local expertise become specific to one environment.
Data lock-in: Prompts, logs, evaluations and historic outputs are difficult to move or reuse elsewhere.

slides.heading: This is path dependence at institutional scale
slides.body: Early choices about tools, contracts and norms do not stay local or temporary. They accumulate and become the pathway through which later decisions are constrained.

notes.transition: Name the mechanism.
points: This is path dependence at institutional scale.
Today's local decisions accumulate into tomorrow's constrained options.
You can use QWERTY here as a light metaphor if helpful, but do not over-explain it.
Standards harden: File formats, APIs, assessment rules and reporting practices settle around early winners.
Investment compounds: Training, integrations and custom workflows make the original choice seem ever more rational.
Alternatives narrow: Institutions eventually face not a free choice, but a high-cost break from their own accumulated history.

slides.heading: Architecture is governance, whether institutions notice or not
slides.body: Law and policy matter, but so do the defaults built into code, market structure and everyday norms. Procurement is therefore not a back-office activity. It is a governance choice.

notes.transition: Make the governance claim explicit.
points: Architecture regulates behaviour whether institutions notice it or not.
This is the place for the short Lessig bridge: code, market structure, norms, and law all matter.
Say plainly that procurement is governance because procurement shapes architecture.
Code: Systems decide what is easy, visible, logged, exportable, contestable or impossible.
Market: Concentration and bundling shape what appears affordable or realistic.
Norms and law: Institutional expectations and regulation matter most when they are translated into architecture and contracts.

slides.text: Preserve the right of exit before convenience becomes culture.

notes.transition: Turn the section into a practical principle.
points: The right of exit has to be protected before convenience becomes culture.
This is not about planning to leave every system; it is about never becoming unable to.
Use it to introduce an architectural implication: modularity and plural ecosystems preserve options before the squeeze arrives.

slides.heading: Prefer modular architecture and plural ecosystems
slides.body: The goal is not to build everything locally. It is to avoid allowing one stack to become the only imaginable path.

notes.transition: Turn the right of exit into an architectural principle.
points: Do not let one stack become the only imaginable route through the institution.
Modularity and plural ecosystems preserve bargaining power, resilience and room to adapt.
This is how you keep options open before convenience hardens into culture.

slides.title: A different strategy
slides.subtitle: Engaged stewardship instead of passive consumption

notes.transition: Now gather the earlier implications into a more coherent strategy.
points: The audience has already seen governance, procurement, data and architecture implications earlier in the talk.
This section consolidates those scattered implications into an explicit stewardship posture.
The answer remains neither refusal nor surrender; it is now being named more fully.

slides.heading: The strongest response is neither refusal nor surrender
slides.body: Universities do not need a fantasy of total sovereignty, and they should not accept passive dependence either. The workable position is engaged stewardship.

notes.transition: State the third position clearly.
points: The strongest response is engaged stewardship.
Say that universities may buy some things, build or host some things, adapt some things, and refuse some things.
The important point is doing that from deliberate governance rather than drift.
Buy some things: Use commercial infrastructure where it is genuinely useful and where the terms are governable.
Build or host selectively: Keep local or sector-controlled options for high-value or high-risk uses where continuity, privacy or contestability matter most.
Refuse some things: Reject deployments that undermine educational purpose even if they look efficient on paper.

slides.heading: Start from public-interest goals, not adoption goals
slides.body: An AI strategy should define what the university is trying to protect and improve before it defines what tools it plans to deploy.

notes.transition: Start the constructive section from purpose.
points: Strategy has to begin with what the university is trying to protect and improve, not with tool adoption targets.
Use human contact, inclusion, and mission as guardrails.
This keeps the policy agenda tied to educational purpose rather than IT modernisation alone.
Human contact: Use AI to create more time for mentoring, feedback, discussion and care.
Inclusion and accessibility: Prioritise translation, captioning, alternative formats and scaffolded support.
Mission guardrail: Explicitly reject headcount reduction or content automation in place of teaching as primary aims.

slides.heading: People adopt AI through motivation, capability and trust
slides.body: Good strategy recognises that use is not governed only by rules. It is governed by why people reach for systems, whether they know how to use them well, and whether the institution gives them reason to trust the arrangements.

notes.transition: Explain adoption more realistically.
points: People reach for AI through motivation, capability, and trust.
Governance fails if it treats use as something controlled only by rules.
This is a useful slide for showing that stewardship includes culture and support, not just restriction.
Motivation: Time pressure, workload, ambiguity and accessibility needs all drive real-world use.
Capability: Training, discipline-specific guidance and verification habits determine whether use is shallow or reflective.
Trust: Clear permissions, transparent data rules and reliable tools reduce covert, anxious or inconsistent use.

slides.heading: Make evaluation a core institutional capability
slides.body: Universities should not rely on product demos, generic benchmarks or marketing claims to decide what is acceptable in teaching and governance.

notes.transition: Then move to evaluation.
points: Evaluation is the quiet centrepiece of stewardship.
Say that universities cannot rely on demos, generic benchmarks, or marketing claims for high-stakes uses.
Stress task-based testing, independent scrutiny, and continuous review.
Task-based testing: Build benchmark tasks for feedback, retrieval, advising, accessibility and disciplinary use cases.
Independent scrutiny: Review hallucination risk, bias, privacy leakage, citation behaviour and fail-safe performance.
Continuous review: Re-evaluate when underlying models, terms or integrations change upstream.

slides.heading: Stewardship is a compact but demanding agenda
slides.body: Universities do not need one hundred principles. They need a small number of capabilities applied consistently.

stats.label: core capabilities: procurement, data governance, evaluation, resilience and collaboration
default questions: what if prices rise, quality drops, or the provider disappears?
governing standard: does this strengthen educational purpose and public value?

notes.transition: Compress the stewardship agenda into something memorable.
points: Five capabilities, three default questions, one governing standard.
Say that institutions do not need one hundred principles; they need a small set of capabilities used consistently.
This slide should make the constructive section feel manageable.

slides.heading: Sector collaboration and public alternatives matter
slides.body: No single university has enough leverage to negotiate every clause, evaluate every model or build every critical service alone. Collaboration is a practical necessity.

notes.transition: Finish the section by scaling outward.
points: Single universities are too small to negotiate, evaluate, and build everything alone.
Sector collaboration and public alternatives are not idealism; they are a realistic response to asymmetric markets.
This lets you sound practical without sounding institutionally isolated.
Shared procurement: Use sector bodies and consortia to negotiate stronger data terms, exit rights and auditability.
Shared evaluation: Build common test suites, model cards and red-teaming practices for educational use cases.
Public-interest infrastructure: Explore national, regional or mission-based alternatives where the market produces choke points.

slides.text: The alternative to passive dependence is not heroic self-sufficiency. It is practical stewardship.

notes.transition: State the positive identity of the argument.
points: The alternative to passive dependence is practical stewardship.
This line matters because it keeps the keynote from sounding like a sovereignty fantasy.
Pause slightly; this is the constructive counterpart to the earlier warnings.

slides.title: What the next decade looks like
slides.subtitle: Two futures are still possible

notes.transition: Move from strategy to consequences.
points: This final section asks what the next decade looks like under two different patterns of choice.
Use the subtitle to frame the end as a genuine fork, not a foregone conclusion.
The tone here should become slightly more civic and decision-oriented.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities play this moment well
slides.body: The optimistic future is not one in which universities beat hyperscalers at their own game. It is one in which they use the boom to build durable, plural and publicly oriented capability.

notes.transition: Paint the better future concretely.
points: A good future is not universities beating hyperscalers at their own game.
It is cheaper infrastructure on better terms, human-centred pedagogy, and stronger public capability.
Keep the picture specific enough to feel plausible rather than utopian.
Cheaper infrastructure, better terms: Institutions inherit mature tools and compute without surrendering all bargaining power.
Human-centred pedagogy: AI is used to deepen feedback, accessibility and high-value contact rather than thin them out.
Stronger public capacity: Sector collaboration creates standards, evaluation routines and alternatives that keep the system contestable.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities get captured by the stack
slides.body: The pessimistic future is not spectacular collapse. It is a quieter form of dependency in which institutions keep functioning while their freedom, pluralism and educational quality erode.

notes.transition: Then contrast it with the quieter bad future.
points: The captured future is not dramatic collapse; it is ongoing functioning under worsening dependency.
Emphasise rent extraction, thinner provision, and opaque governance.
The point is to show what slow loss of autonomy looks like in ordinary institutional life.
Rent extraction: AI becomes a permanent premium layer across teaching, admin, assessment and research workflows.
Thin provision: Lower-status students and institutions get automated, standardised provision while wealthier parts of the sector preserve human-rich models.
Opaque governance: Research, pedagogy and student support are increasingly shaped by systems the university cannot fully audit or challenge.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: The next few years require three decisions from leaders
slides.body: Every institution does not need the same answer, but all of them need to decide what they will refuse to outsource by default.

points.title: What must we retain control over?
What should we build or evaluate together?

notes.transition: Convert the contrast into leadership choices.
points: Every institution has to decide what it will refuse to outsource by default.
Use the three questions as decision tests: what must we retain control over, what should we build or evaluate together, and what rights should students have?
This sets up the closing agenda cleanly.
What must we retain control over?: Define the teaching, assessment, data or support functions where exit, auditability and human oversight are non-negotiable.
What should we build or evaluate together?: Identify the areas where sector collaboration creates more leverage than acting alone.
What rights should students have?: Make explicit the rights to transparency, contestability, accessibility and non-AI pathways in core processes.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: A Monday-morning agenda for institutions
slides.body: The first moves are not glamorous. They are the foundations of a university that remains able to govern its own AI future.

notes.transition: Make the end operational.
points: The Monday-morning agenda shows that stewardship begins with unglamorous but doable steps.
Map dependence, tighten the basics, and act collectively.
This is where the talk leaves the audience with something they can do next week.
Map dependence: Identify where AI is already embedded in teaching, assessment, support, analytics and procurement.
Tighten the basics: Create a cross-functional procurement checklist, clear data rules and one evaluation routine for a high-priority use case.
Act collectively: Join or build a coalition for shared bargaining, evaluation or open alternatives before the defaults settle.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.text: Universities are not only deciding whether to use AI. They are helping fix the defaults of higher education for years.

notes.transition: Lift back from the checklist to the stakes.
points: The point is bigger than whether to deploy a particular tool.
Universities are helping set the defaults that will structure higher education for years.
This is the right slide to widen the frame before the final question.

slides.text: Who will ultimately control the AI infrastructure higher education comes to depend on — and what kind of university will that make possible?

notes.transition: End with the question rather than another answer.
points: Let the closing question hang for a moment and hand neatly into discussion or panel.
The point is to force the room to connect infrastructure control with the kind of university it wants to make possible.
Do not rush the last line; it is the civic and institutional landing point.
